sourceMovie.title: Yojimbo
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Yojimbo for the same reason it broke through in 1961: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a thriller rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Akira Kurosawa keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The drama and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Spy Who Came In from the Cold
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Yojimbo clicked for you, The Spy Who Came In from the Cold (1965) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Seven Samurai
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Yojimbo clicked for you, Seven Samurai (1954) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Yojimbo?
faq.answer: Try The Spy Who Came In from the Cold, Seven Samurai, 47 Ronin—each shares DNA with Yojimbo while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Yojimbo?
faq.answer: Yojimbo (1961) is best described as Drama, Thriller. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Yojimbo part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Sanjuro Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
